results.title: Lifecycle of a Durable Object - Cloudflare Docs
excerpts:
Section Title: Lifecycle of a Durable Object > Shutdown behavior > Working without shutdown hooks
Content:
Durable Objects may shut down due to deployments, inactivity, or runtime decisions. Rather than relying on shutdown hooks (which are not provided), design your application to write state incrementally.
Shutdown hooks or lifecycle callbacks that run before shutdown are not provided because Cloudflare cannot guarantee these hooks would execute in all cases, and external software may rely too heavily on these (unreliable) hooks.
Instead of relying on shutdown hooks, you can regularly write to storage to recover gracefully from shutdowns.
For example, if you are processing a stream of data and need to save your progress, write your position to storage as you go rather than waiting to persist it at the end:
JavaScript

Section Title: Meet A2A: Google’s Agent-to-Agent Protocol Explained for Developers > How A2A Works (Step by Step)
Content:
Throughout this exchange, A2A ensures both agents stay **in sync** on the task state. The task has a clear lifecycle and each side knows whether the task is pending, waiting for input, or done. This is a big improvement over ad-hoc agent integrations where you might not know if the other side is still working or got stuck. With A2A, it’s part of the protocol to communicate status and even to push notifications if something changes (agents can optionally send webhook callbacks).

Section Title: Multi-Platform Webhook Triggers UPDATED > Solution
Content:
**Notion webhooks** : Fire on page/database modifications
Document status changes (draft → "ready for review")
Request for Comment (RFC) workflows
Auto-assign reviewers based on topic
In-line commenting with suggestions
**Slack messages** : Bot responds in channels
Public channel responses
Private channel support (with careful logging)
Channel creation triggers for auto-joining
Default prompts for new channels (respond only when mentioned)
**Jira webhooks** : Issue lifecycle events
Issue creation, updates, comments
Custom routing logic for ticket assignment
**Slack reacji (emoji reactions)** : Quick workflow initiation
Listen for specific emojis in any channel
Example: `:jira:` reaction turns thread into ticket
Centralized routing instructions
**Scheduled events** : Periodic triggers
Slack workflows publishing to channels
GitHub Actions cron jobs
```
# Trigger flow
Platform Event → Webhook → Agent Trigger → Workflow Execution
```

results.title: Scale Agentic Automation with A2A, MCP, and Enhanced Connectors with Camunda 8.9-alpha2 | Camunda
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Camunda and A2A
Content:
With our new A2A connectors, Camunda offers a reliable and observable way for a *primary* agent to delegate to a *specialist* , wait for the results, handle timeouts, and escalate when required. The [A2A protocol and A2A Client connectors with Camunda](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/next/reference/announcements-release-notes/890/890-release-notes/) provide that pattern in BPMN: discover capabilities, send a task, and receive results through a transport between agents that fits your network reality.
Essentially, with the A2A protocol, Camunda can now provide agents that communicate with each other directly as well as agents that request specific tasks from a set of tools—essentially, providing our customers with the best of both worlds.
In order to take advantage of A2A with Camunda, you will need to ensure a couple prerequisites are in place:
Access to an A2A-compliant agent
Its associated [Agent Card URL](https://a2a-protocol.org/v0.3.0/specification/)
Section Title: ... > Camunda and A2A
Content:
The Agent Card URL explains what the remote agent can do and how to communicate with it. Then Camunda dispatches a task message from the process and chooses how results should be returned.
In 8.9.0‑alpha2, there are two client connectors. You’ll need to pick the one that best suits your requirements. Camunda supports both a webhook connector pattern as well the more traditional polling approach (ie, a job worker pulling work on an interval).
[](https://camunda.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/image1-3.png)
 ... 
Section Title: ... > A2A Client (polling)
Content:
This method is appropriate when the remote system cannot or will not provide a callback. In this pattern, Camunda initiates work via an outbound call or places a job on a queue and then periodically checks the status until the task is completed. This is a good fit for long-running specialist work—for example, complex RAG analysis or batch enrichment—where callbacks are blocked, unsupported, or unreliable.
As mentioned, Camunda starts the agent by making an outbound call. For example:
**HTTP/gRPC** : POST /jobs to create work and receive a job ID.
**Messaging/queue:** Publish a job message; the agent pulls and starts work.
**Object store trigger:** Upload an input artifact; the agent watches the bucket and begins processing.
Camunda then polls a status endpoint, queue, or artifact location until completion, timeout, or error.
Section Title: ... > A2A Client (polling)
Content:
With this pattern, poll intervals and overall timeouts live in the BPMN model, so the process—not the agent—owns service levels. This means that the correlation is explicit; you pair your retry/backoff logic with a BPMN boundary timer that routes to a compensation path, so the primary agent never blocks.
Polling is the right choice when you can start the job outbound but can’t accept (or trust) callbacks. You kick off the work, then you check back—on your terms, within your time budget.
Section Title: ... > A2A Client (webhook)
Content:
Camunda also exposes an endpoint via the [A2A Client webhook connector](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/next/components/early-access/alpha/a2a-client/a2a-client-polling-connector/) , and the external system calls when the AI job is complete or an event happens. Using this webhook makes the round‑trip event‑driven. You dispatch once, the agent posts results to your callback endpoint, and your process continues immediately on receipt. The webhook approach ensures that the agent is called only once.
Camunda runs this pattern in a fully event-driven way. Your orchestrated process dispatches the job once and includes a correlation key. The external system (or specialist agent) calls the webhook endpoint when the AI task completes or when a notable event occurs (eg, partial results, human-in-the-loop needed, final outcome). On callback receipt, Camunda immediately resumes the correct process instance, so latency comes down to network plus verification time.
Section Title: ... > A2A Client (webhook)
Content:
With this approach, you eliminate polling noise and get a natural fit for elastic workloads. You are able to stop constant checking and let the system respond only when something happens. That cuts wasted compute and avoids traffic spikes during busy periods. It’s a great fit for time-sensitive steps—like fraud checks, real-time offers, or quick AI summaries—where you want the next action to start as soon as the result is ready. It also works well when your internal teams or vendors can send a secure callback to a preapproved address.
The webhook method is appropriate when your flow with latency-sensitive tasks should progress as soon as ready. It is also a good choice when you are building more real-time, event-driven agentic orchestration patterns. Or, when internal specialist agents or vendors are able to sign and deliver callbacks to an allowlisted endpoint.

results.title: Show HN: Webhook Skills – Agent skills for webhook providers and best practices | Hacker News
excerpts:
||[](https://news.ycombinator.com) |**Hacker News** new | past | comments |  |  |  |  | |
| --- | --- | --- | |
| --- |
|| | |[Show HN: Webhook Skills – Agent skills for webhook providers and best practices](https://github.com/hookdeck/webhook-skills) ( github.com/hookdeck ) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| |9 points by leggetter 22 days ago | hide | [past](https://hn.algolia.com/?query=Show%20HN%3A%20Webhook%20Skills%20%E2%80%93%20Agent%20skills%20for%20webhook%20providers%20and%20best%20practices&type=story&dateRange=all&sort=byDate&storyText=false&prefix&page=0) | favorite | 1 comment |
| |I built a collection of webhook skills because AI coding agents are surprisingly bad at webhook integrations. The generated code looks reasonable until you run it, then signature verification fails, raw body handling is wrong, or the middleware order breaks everything.
PostHog's research on LLM code generation ( https://posthog.com/blog/correct-llm-code-generation ) found that agents produce more reliable code when referencing known-working examples rather than reconstructing from training data. That's the approach here.
`webhook-skills` is a collection of provider-specific webhook implementations and best practices guides built on the Agent Skills spec (agentskills.io):
```
  - Runnable examples (currently Express, Next.js, FastAPI, with more frameworks coming)
  - Signature verification with provider-specific gotchas documented
  - Best-practice patterns: idempotency, error handling, retry logic
  - 11 providers at launch (Stripe, Shopify, GitHub, OpenAI, Clerk, Paddle, others), expanding based on my needs or requests.
```
Example:
```
  # list skills
  npx skills add hookdeck/webhook-skills --list

  # install skills
  npx skills add hookdeck/webhook-skills --skill stripe-webhooks --skill webhook-handler-patterns
```

results.title: Google Agent to Agent(A2A) Communication Patterns and When to Use What
excerpts:
Section Title: Google Agent to Agent(A2A) Communication Patterns and When to Use What
Content:
[](https://substack.com/@kenhuangus)
[Ken Huang](https://substack.com/@kenhuangus)
Sep 06, 2025
9
[](https://kenhuangus.substack.com/p/google-agent-to-agenta2a-communication/comments) 2
Share
Google A2A gives you four ways agents can communicate—sync, async polling, SSE, and webhooks. This post will show you when to use each one and how they differ.
 ... 
Section Title: ... > 4. The "Call Me Back": Push Notifications with Webhooks
Content:
For extremely long-running or resource-intensive tasks, even keeping an SSE connection open might be impractical. Webhooks provide the most resource-efficient solution by reversing the roles: the client asks the server to call it back when the job is done.
Section Title: ... > 4. The "Call Me Back": Push Notifications with Webhooks
Content:
**How it Works:** The client sends a request to start a task and includes a special URL (a "webhook"). The server acknowledges the request, and the client can completely disconnect. Once the task is finished (which could be hours later), the server sends a single, asynchronous notification (a "push") to the registered webhook URL with the final result. **Real-World Analogy:** Dropping off your car at a garage for a major repair. You give the mechanic your phone number and leave. You don't call them every hour, nor do you wait at the garage. When the car is ready, the mechanic calls you. **When to Use It:** This is the go-to pattern for heavyweight, asynchronous processes. Examples include training a machine learning model, processing a large dataset, or orchestrating a complex, multi-step workflow.
 ... 
Section Title: ... > Choosing the Right Tool for the Job
Content:
There is no single "best" communication pattern. The optimal choice depends entirely on the nature of the task.
For a quick, blocking query? Use **Synchronous Request/Response** .
For a medium-length task where the client can check back later? Use **Asynchronous Polling** .
For real-time, continuous updates from a server? Use **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** .
For a very long-running, fire-and-forget task? Use **Push Notifications (Webhooks)** .
